# Architecture 2.0 Resource Links {#sec-appendix-d-architecture-20-resources}

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This appendix collects living links for the Architecture 2.0 preview edition and adjacent resources that are useful for building, evaluating, or teaching represented architecture design loops.}
```

These links are not a general computer-architecture directory. A resource earns
space here only if it helps an Architecture 2.0 loop name a task, represent
state, expose actions, return feedback, preserve evidence, or reject a result.
Use the list as a starting point and check current versions before relying on
any benchmark, dataset, simulator, or tool.
This directory uses live links rather than formal bibliography entries; cite the
primary paper or project artifact when making a scholarly claim.

## Architecture 2.0 Framing

- **Course:** [CS249r: Architecture 2.0](https://harvard-edge.github.io/cs249r_fall2025/).
  Teaching sequence and paper-discussion structure for turning Architecture 2.0
  from framing into exercises, cards, and project reviews.

- **Essay:** [Architecture 2.0 gymnasium essay](https://www.sigarch.org/architecture-2-0-why-computer-architects-need-a-data-centric-ai-gymnasium/).
  Intent-level framing for why architecture needs data-centric environments
  rather than isolated model demonstrations.

- **Foundations article:** [IEEE Computer article](https://www.computer.org/csdl/magazine/co/2025/02/10857820/23VCdDhsEAo).
  Background article for the Architecture 2.0 vision, autonomy levels, and
  agent roles in modern computer system design.

- **Workshop:** [ISCA 2026 Architecture 2.0 workshop](https://harvard-edge.github.io/isca-26-arch-2-workshop/).
  Community feedback loop for preview-edition claims, examples, benchmarks, and
  shared artifacts.

- **Portal:** [Architecture 2.0 resource site](https://sites.google.com/g.harvard.edu/arch2/home).
  Companion material and update surface for links that move faster than the
  book.

## Architecture 2.0 Templates and Artifacts

- **Design-loop card and review rubric:** @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card.
  Use the card to review a paper, proposal, classroom project, or internal loop
  before trusting its architectural claim.

- **Research paper recipe card:** @tbl-research-paper-recipe-card.
  Use the recipe to state claim boundaries, workloads, baselines, evidence,
  negative traces, rejection authority, and human decision ownership.

- **Blank design-loop template:** @tbl-blank-design-loop-card.
  Use the blank card as a one-page handout for paper reviews, project proposals,
  and design reviews.

- **Resource role checklist:** @sec-appendix-c-resource-catalog.
  Use the catalog to decide whether a benchmark, tool, dataset, or artifact
  actually makes the loop more representable, actionable, measurable, or
  auditable.

## Roadmaps and Evidence Standards

- **IRDS Systems and Architectures:** [IRDS roadmap area](https://irds.ieee.org/irds-systems-and-architectures/).
  System-to-device roadmap that connects application drivers, system
  requirements, architecture trends, and technology constraints. Use it as a
  model for Architecture 2.0 roadmap plots that show capability gaps, evidence
  needs, and maturity horizons rather than only current tool lists.

- **IRDS Applications Benchmarking:** [IRDS editions](https://irds.ieee.org/editions/).
  Application-driven benchmarking roadmap. Use it as a reference point when a
  loop claims that a workload, scenario, or benchmark suite is representative
  enough to support a broader architecture decision.

## Architecture Reasoning and Design-Problem Benchmarks

- **QuArch:** [quarch.ai](https://quarch.ai/).
  Architecture question-answering and reasoning benchmark. Use it to test
  whether a model can reason over architecture concepts, but not as evidence
  that a loop can act through tools or reject candidates.

- **CVDP benchmark:** [NVlabs/cvdp_benchmark](https://github.com/NVlabs/cvdp_benchmark)
  and [Hugging Face dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/nvidia/cvdp-benchmark-dataset).
  Comprehensive Verilog design problems for RTL design and verification. Use it
  when the loop claim involves HDL generation, test harnesses, simulation
  feedback, or verification failures.

- **VerilogEval:** [NVlabs/verilog-eval](https://github.com/NVlabs/verilog-eval).
  Specification-to-RTL and Verilog code-generation benchmark with executable
  checks. Use it for method-role claims about RTL generation and compile/test
  feedback, not for system-level architecture claims.

- **KernelBench:** [ScalingIntelligence/KernelBench](https://github.com/ScalingIntelligence/KernelBench).
  GPU-kernel generation benchmark with correctness and performance evaluation.
  Use it to study software-loop feedback, codegen, and performance evidence
  before claiming architecture-level benefit.

## Architecture Environments and Design-Space Exploration

- **ArchGym:** [Architecture Gym](https://github.com/srivatsankrishnan/oss-arch-gym).
  Environment interface for ML-assisted architecture design. Use it as a
  concrete example of actions, observations, costs, and feedback in a bounded
  architecture loop.

- **Timeloop:** [NVlabs/timeloop](https://github.com/NVlabs/timeloop).
  Mapping and modeling tool for tensor workloads on
  accelerator architectures. Use it for dataflow, mapping, memory hierarchy,
  and accelerator design-space loops.

- **Accelergy:** [Accelergy](https://github.com/Accelergy-Project/accelergy).
  Energy-estimation infrastructure for accelerators. Use it when a loop needs
  an explicit energy feedback source and calibration boundary.

- **MAESTRO:** [maestro-project/maestro](https://github.com/maestro-project/maestro).
  Analytical cost model for DNN dataflows and tiling. Use it as a fast-feedback
  model whose limits must be recorded before higher commitment.

## Full-System Simulation and Hardware/Software Harnesses

- **gem5:** [gem5 simulator](https://www.gem5.org/).
  Modular computer-system simulator. Use it for architecture feedback that
  needs workload execution, timing behavior, and reproducible simulator state.

- **FireSim:** [FireSim](https://fires.im/).
  FPGA-accelerated full-system simulation. Use it when the loop needs stronger
  hardware/software feedback than a software-only proxy can provide.

- **Chipyard:** [Chipyard docs](https://chipyard.readthedocs.io/).
  Integrated framework for generating and evaluating hardware systems. Use it
  when the loop must connect generators, RTL, simulation, and implementation
  artifacts.

## Physical-Design and EDA Evidence

- **OpenROAD:** [OpenROAD project](https://theopenroadproject.org/).
  Open-source RTL-to-GDS flow. Use it for loops that need physical-design
  feedback, timing/area/power evidence, or signoff-adjacent rejection.

- **CircuitNet:** [CircuitNet](https://github.com/circuitnet/CircuitNet).
  VLSI CAD dataset for machine-learning applications in EDA. Use it for
  cross-stage prediction and physical-design learning claims, while preserving
  tool provenance and task scope.

- **ChiPBench:** [AI chip-placement benchmark](https://openreview.net/forum?id=gDkQ5iesrI).
  Benchmark focused on end-to-end physical-design impact for AI chip placement.
  Use it when placement evidence must be tied to downstream physical metrics,
  not only intermediate scores.

## Workload and Benchmark Governance

- **XRBench:** [XRBench paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.08675).
  Extended-reality machine-learning benchmark suite. Use it as a workload anchor
  for mobile-XR architecture loops, including scenario definition and workload
  coverage questions.

- **MLCommons benchmarks:** [MLCommons benchmarks](https://mlcommons.org/benchmarks/).
  Benchmark governance and reporting infrastructure. Use it as a model for
  workload versions, run rules, comparability, and community-maintained evidence
  rather than as a generic performance leaderboard.

## Evidence, Provenance, and Reproducibility Practice

- **ML reproducibility checklists:** [NeurIPS reproducibility program](https://neurips.cc/Conferences/2021/PaperInformation/PaperChecklist).
  Checklist culture for making assumptions, experiments, compute, and evidence
  explicit. Use it as an adjacent-field template when writing Architecture 2.0
  evidence ledgers.

- **Papers with Code:** [Papers with Code](https://paperswithcode.com/).
  Public convention for linking papers, tasks, datasets, and code. Use it as a
  reminder that architecture needs comparable task and artifact metadata, not
  only isolated results.

- **Software Heritage:** [Software Heritage archive](https://www.softwareheritage.org/).
  Long-term source-code archival infrastructure. Use it when provenance matters
  more than a mutable repository link.

## Teaching and Review Artifacts

- **Nand2Tetris:** [nand2tetris.org](https://www.nand2tetris.org/).
  Project-centered computing-systems pedagogy. Use it as a reference pattern for
  Architecture 2.0 exercises where every abstraction earns its place.

- **The Turing Way:** [The Turing Way](https://the-turing-way.netlify.app/).
  Community handbook for reproducible, ethical, and collaborative data science.
  Use it as an adjacent model for turning local research practice into shared
  reviewable process.

## Case Studies to Build

- **Learned chip placement:** Pair @sec-feedback-verification-trust with
  @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack to read placement claims as evidence ledgers:
  task, baseline, tool flow, physical metrics, rejection authority, and
  independent reproducibility.

- **Architecture gymnasiums:** Pair @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces
  with @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization to read ArchGym-style
  work as an environment contract: actions, observations, costs, and what can
  reject a result.

- **Kernel and code-generation loops:** Pair @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack
  with KernelBench-style resources to ask when a software-loop result becomes
  architecture evidence and when it remains only local code evidence.

## Using and Citing the Preview

Use this preview as a working vocabulary for paper reviews, project proposals,
class discussions, and internal design reviews. For citation, cite the preview
URL and version when referring to this book directly, and cite the Architecture
2.0 foundations article when referring to the broader vision and autonomy
framing. For feedback on the preview, use the
[ISCA 2026 Architecture 2.0 workshop](https://harvard-edge.github.io/isca-26-arch-2-workshop/),
the [Architecture 2.0 resource site](https://sites.google.com/g.harvard.edu/arch2/home),
or the [arch2 repository issues](https://github.com/harvard-edge/arch2/issues).
